coredata.dc:title: Facile and green synthesis of N, Cl-dual-doped carbon dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for hematin and temperature sensing
coredata.dc:creator: He, Wei
Huo, Zhenxiang
Sun, Xiangying
Shen, Jiangshan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we made use of a one-step hydrothermal method for the purpose of synthesizing the multifunctional N, Cl co-doped carbon dots (NCl-CDs) from hexadecylpyridinium chloride as raw materials. The as-prepared NCl-CDs as a fluorescent probe have been put to use to develop a label free fluorescence sensing platform to detect hematin and temperature. The NCl-CDs displays temperature-dependent fluorescence that has a sensational linear feedback between 20 and 80 ℃. Moreover, hematin makes the effective quenching of the fluorescence intensity of NCl-CDs possible on the basis of inner filter effect (IFE). This sensing platform possessed elevated selectivity towards hematin with the detection limit amounting to 0.16 μM, which was further applicable in detecting hematin in human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hematin
Temperature
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity of NCl-CDs for hematin detection
Investigation of quenching mechanism for hematin

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foundation of Graphene Powder & Composite Research Center of Fujian Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The current research work received the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21275059 and 21575044 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (No. 2015J01054 and 2016J01062 ), Foundation of Graphene Powder & Composite Research Center of Fujian Province (No. 2017H2001), and Subsidized Project for Postgraduates’ Innovative Fund in Scientific Research of Huaqiao University (No. 18011081004).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (Inset graph shows HRTEM image of CDs) and (B) Size distribution of the as-synthesised NCl-CDs.
(A) XPS survey scan of NCl-CDs, (B) C1 s XPS, (C) N1 s XPS and (D) Cl2p XPS.
(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra, excitation and emission spectra of NCl-CDs. (B) PL spectra of NCl-CDs with different excitation wavelength.

Change of hydrated particle size of carbon dots during heating (A) and cooling (B). (C) F/F0-temperture plots of NCl-CDs during heating and cooling mechanisms (the fluorescence severity at a temperature of 20°C as the standard, F0). (D) The cycle experiment of the temperature response over NCl-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCl-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of hematin in 10 mM BR buffer solution (pH 7). (B) The relationship curve between F0/F and concentration of hematin.

(A) Selectivity of NCl-CDs to hematin (10 μM) and different materials (100 μM). NCl-CDs was fixed to be blank. (B) The fluorescence intensity ratios of NCl-CDs system when hematin (NCl-CDs are present with the addition of 10 μM of hematin, blank) with varying coexistent substances (100 μM). (Fm,0/Fm, Fm,0 and Fm indicate the fluorescence intensities of NCl-CDs system in the absence as well as the existence of anyone of the varying coexistent substances, correspondingly).

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of hematin, and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NCl-CDs. (B) Fluorescence decay curves of NCl-CDs alone and NCl-CDs with hematin of different concentration. (C) The LUMO and HOMO of NCl-CDs and hematin. (D) Suppressed efficiency (E%) of observed (black) and corrected (red) measurements for NCl-CDs subsequent to the each addition of different concentrations of hematin. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Scheme of synthetic strategy for NCl-CDs and the principle of hematin and temperature sensing.
Detection of hematin of human serum samples by this present method.

head.ce:title: Facile and green synthesis of N, Cl-dual-doped carbon dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for hematin and temperature sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Huaqiao University, Key Laboratory of Molecular Designing and Green Conversions Fujian University, 361021 Xiamen, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Key Laboratory of Molecular Designing and Green Conversions Fujian University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Huaqiao University, Key Laboratory of Molecular Designing and Green Conversions Fujian University, 361021 Xiamen, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we made use of a one-step hydrothermal method for the purpose of synthesizing the multifunctional N, Cl co-doped carbon dots (NCl-CDs) from hexadecylpyridinium chloride as raw materials. The as-prepared NCl-CDs as a fluorescent probe have been put to use to develop a label free fluorescence sensing platform to detect hematin and temperature. The NCl-CDs displays temperature-dependent fluorescence that has a sensational linear feedback between 20 and 80 ℃. Moreover, hematin makes the effective quenching of the fluorescence intensity of NCl-CDs possible on the basis of inner filter effect (IFE). This sensing platform possessed elevated selectivity towards hematin with the detection limit amounting to 0.16 μM, which was further applicable in detecting hematin in human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Hematin, iron (III) protoporphyrin IX chloride, refers to a natural iron supplement, playing a pivotal part in the oxygen regulation systems [1,2]. It has been reported as having been put to use not only in medicine, but also biology, food and other fields [3–5]. Nonetheless, excessive hematin, which could be embedded into lipid membranes, besides catalysing the formation of hydroxyl radicals and oxidizing membrane components, accordingly promoting the cell lysis and death, is deemed as harmful [6,7]. That is why the fast-paced detection of hematin is of critical significance. Until now, traditionally detecting hematin, involving the high-performance liquid chromatography [8], electrochemical [9,10], mass spectrometry [11,12], and absorption spectrophotometry [13], has been reported. The majority of these methodologies, nonetheless, needs costly tools or having low sensitivity. As a result, developing a straightforward, affordable, sensitive, and fast-paced methodology of hematin determination is of immense significance. Fluorescence analysis, being a pivotal analytical tool, has been extensively applied for its simple operation, fast and high sensitivity [14].

Temperature, as an essential physiological indicator, indicates the life activities of living organisms [15]. That is why the monitoring of temperature in vivo is has specific importance. At present, there have been developed a number of fluorescent materials in order to detecting temperature, for instance, semiconductor quantum dots [16], metal-organic frameworks [17], and fluorescent dyes [18]. However, these materials have high toxicity as well as low stability, accordingly limiting their further application [19].

Currently, carbon dots (CDs) has drawn much attention for convenient modification, coupled with the high-photochemical stability, low toxicity, excellent water solubility, and reasonable biocompatibility in comparison with the conventional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes [20–23]. Therefore, CDs has unlimited applications in sensors [24–26], bioimaging [27–33], photocatalysis [34–36], and drug delivery [37]. In the past few years, many methodologies have seen successful developments to synthesize CDs that include hydrothermal methods [38–41], microwave assisted methods [42,43], ultrasonic assisted methods [44,45], and electrochemical methods [46,47], and so on. Among these methodologies, the hydrothermal methodology has broad applications for the synthesis of CDs owing to its simplicity, quickness, and environmental friendliness. The inner filter effect (IFE) refers to a mechanism, wherein the excitation or emission spectrum of the phosphor overlays with the absorption spectrum of the light absorber in order to cause the fluorescence quenching of phosphor [48]. This impact gives rise to detection errors in fluorescence analysis. Therefore, initially, researchers placed emphasis on investigating the correction of the IFE. In the year 2009, Shang's group discovered the fact that metal nanoparticles possess a reasonable absorption coefficient that could be put to use as sound absorbers [49]. Since then, the IFE has gradually evolved into a new analytical methodology [50,51].

In the present research work, a simple and green methodology to the synthesis of NCl-CDs by hydrothermal mechanism has been put forward (Scheme 1). The NCl-CDs, being a productive temperature indicator, manifests reasonable temperature-dependent fluorescence that has a sensational linear feedback between 20 ℃ and 80 ℃. Together with that, hematin makes possible the effective quenching of the fluorescence of the NCl-CDs on the basis of IFE, which is applicable for detecting hematin in human serum specimens.

The purchase of hematin and hexadecylpyridinium chloride (CPC) was made from Aladdin (Shanghai, China), besides purchasing other reagents from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The preparation of ultrapure water was done with the help of a Milli-Q water purification system (USA).

The preparation of CDs was carried out through the hydrothermal treatment of cetylpyridinium chloride (CPC). Synthetic conditions can be optimized to see Fig. S1. With regard to a normal procedure, 0.5 g of CPC was added into 20 mL water, followed by transferring the mixture into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and then heating a temperature of 190°C for a period of 6 h. Subsequent to heating, the solution gradually changes from a colourless appearance to a colour of light yellow, indicating the formation of the NCl-CDs. The purification of the NCl-CDs was carried out with the use of a dialysis tube (500 Da, molecular weight cut off) for a period of approximately 48 h. Moreover, the final NCl-CDs was stored at a temperature of 4 ℃ for further investigations.

The use of a standard three-electrode system was made for the measurement of the electrochemical performance of the prepared NCl-CDs. The platinum plate was served as an auxiliary electrode. In addition, the use of Ag/AgCl was made as a reference electrode, besides using glassy carbon electrode as working electrode. Through the use of cyclic voltammetry with the latent sweeping between −1.0 V and 1.0 V at a scan rate of 0.5 V/s. ECL signals of the prepared NCl-CDs (0.1 mg/mL) in the electrolyte that contained the phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (0.1 M, pH 7).

Aimed at investigating the fluorescence quenching association with hematin to the prepared NCl-CDs, the fluorescence of the prepared NCl-CDs in the existence of different kinds of concentrations of hematin. In the specific experimental mechanism, 10 mg/mL NCl-CDs stock solution and 500 μL BR buffer (10 mM, pH 7) were mixed with the varying concentrations of hematin, and eventually, the volume was brought to 5 mL. Thereafter, the changes of fluorescence spectra were recorded with a 370 nm excitation wavelength. For investigating the selectivity and anti-interference capability of the fluorescence sensing system, the selection of the concentration of interfering substances was made as 100 μM.

As evident from the TEM image of NCl-CDs (Fig. 1 ), the prepared NCl-CDs resemble spherical particles, whereas the overall distribution is uniform with the particle size of 3.63 nm averagely. Meanwhile, the HRTEM picture of CDs makes a clear indication of the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were employed for showing the surface elements as well as the chemical bonds of CDs. As evident from the XPS full spectrum (Fig. 2 A), CDs consist of not just C, but also N, O and Cl atoms, of which C element possesses 86.64%, N element contains 4.00%, O element contains 4.93%, and Cl element contains 4.43%. Moreover, the four peaks at 284.5, 339.6, 198.9, and 534.1 eV were ascribed to not just C1s, but also N1s, Cl2p3, and O1s, correspondingly. As the finding apparently illustrates, there is the doping of N as well as Cl on the façade of the NCl-CDs. As presented by the fine C spectrum (Fig. 2B), significant peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.5 eV, and 288.1 eV were assigned to CC, COC, and OCO, correspondingly. The Cl 2p spectrum (Fig. 2D) contains two bands of 196.9 as well as 198.5 eV, attributed to chlorine in both of the cohorts, such as CCl and NCl, correspondingly. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2C) suggests that nitrogen is predominantly available in the form of CNC (401.1 eV). The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2) was possible to be convolved into two significant peaks at 531.4 eV and 531.9 eV, which represented the existence of CO and COH. In the meantime, the FTIR spectrum of the prepared NCl-CDs was recorded (Fig. S3). In addition, the peak at 3383 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH. The characteristic absorption band at 3136 cm−1 stems from the stretching vibration of NH. The peaks at 2913 cm−1 and 2855 cm−1 are symmetrical and opposed to CH2 . The absorption peaks at both 1710 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 are the representations of CO and the CC stretching vibration absorption; 1174 cm−1 is termed as the characteristic stretching vibration absorption of the absorption peak CO. The 779 cm−1 absorption peak is an indication of the CCl stretching vibration absorption. To summarize, the FTIR findings are observed as in lien with the XPS data, which clearly suggest the successful incorporation of nitrogen and chlorine into CDs.

There were observed absorption peaks at 255 and 400 nm for NCl-CDs in Fig. 3 A. moreover, an absorption peak at 255 nm that corresponded to CPC, which suggested that the surface of NCl-CDs still retains some functional cohorts of CPC. In addition, the optimal emission peak of the prepared NCl-CDs at 370 nm excitation wavelength has its location at 460 nm (Fig. 3B). Apparently, the prepared NCl-CDs has excitation dependence, whereas red shift takes place at the position of the emission peak subjected to the conditions of varying excitation wavelengths. The calculation of the quantum yield and the average fluorescence lifetime of the NCl-CDs was carried out as 7.01 percent and 5.219 ns, correspondingly (Figs. S4 and S5). The as-prepared NCl-CDs, which indicates outstanding photostability with regard to the fluorescence intensity, continues to be invariant subjected to the continued excitation (350 nm) with a UV lamp for a period of sixty minutes (Fig. S6A). In the meantime, the fluorescence intensity of NCl-CDs undergoes a slight variation despite that the concentrations of NaCl and H2O2 approach as high as 1 M, respectively (Fig. S6B and S6D), which shed light on the fact that NCl-CDs is likely to show much stability in a medium that has relatively high ionic robustness or subjected to the oxidation condition. Besides that, the prepared NCl-CDs has good light stability subjected to acidic conditions (Fig. S6C).

In the current work, the NCl-CDs manifest outstanding temperature response characteristics all through the heating and cooling process, which has been demonstrated in Fig. S7. Together with that, Fig. 4 A and B shed light on the hydrated particle size of NCl-CDs at varying temperatures. It was discovered that the hydration particle size of the NCl-CDs emerges as larger with the increase in the temperature, which indicates that the temperature rise gives rise to the NCl-CDs to aggregate, causing the fluorescence quenching of the NCl-CDs. Nonetheless, with the decline in the temperature, the hydration particle size of NCl-CDs starts declining, which indicates that the cooling has the potential of causing NCl-CDs to depolymerize. Apparently, the NCl-CDs as a temperature probe indicates a reversible temperature response, in addition to a reasonable linear association (Fig. 4C). A cyclic operation was put to use for the purpose of evaluating the stability of the fluorescence severity of the NCl-CDs. Fig. 4D sheds light on the fact that the fluorescence severity of the NCl-CDs was hardly changed subsequent to the five cycles of repetition, indicating the high stability of hat the temperature-dependent fluorescence severity of the NCl-CDs.

For the purpose of obtaining the outstanding sensitivity for the detection of hematin, different detection conditions were investigated. The experimental findings highlight the fact that pH at 7, 10 mg/mL NCl-CDs, and response time of 1 min were observed as having the topmost sensitivity (Fig. S8). Subjected to the best conditions, the fluorescence severity of the prepared NCl-CDs was quenched by hematin. In the meantime, Fig. 5 revealed that there exists a reasonable linear association between F0/F and hematin concentration that ranged from 1.0 μM to 32.0 μM with the LOD of 0.16 μM, and the standard curve could be expressed as F0/F = 0.2151C+0.8304, where F0 and F represent NCl-CDs in the absence and presence of hematin and C is the concentration of hematin (μM). Meanwhile, comparing the previous methods for detecting hematin, we can see that our method is fast and reliable and has high sensitivity (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of NCl-CDs for hematin detection
ce:section.ce:para: Aimed at further exploring the selectivity of NCl-CDs to detect hematin, the evaluation of the impacts of some interfering substances on the fluorescence quenching of NCl-CDs was carried out at same conditions, correspondingly. As evident from Fig. 6 A, the sensor system possesses outstanding selectivity for hematin, even though the concentration of other substances were 10 times that of hematin. Together with that, some of the above materials have been chosen for investigating the intrusion of hematin detection on the basis of the NCl-CDs owing to the fact that they are likely to be the latent interfering materials in the actual specimens. Subsequent to that, we performed the recording of the fluorescence spectra of the NCl-CDs/hematin system (Chematin = 10 μM) in the presence of anyone of interfering materials (their concentrations are maintained at 100 μM), correspondingly. Moreover, the corresponding fluorescence quenching ratios are presented in Fig. 6B, which suggest that the intruding materials have insignificant impacts on detecting hematin by NCl-CDs. All of the results suggest that the methodology manifests selectivity as well as sensitivity with regard to the monitoring of hematin.

The fluorescence sensing system is further application for detecting hematin in blood specimens. We took two normal serum samples, followed by diluting 50-fold with the BR buffer (10 mM, pH 7) prior to the use. The experimental results are presented in Table 1 . The recovery from 98.38% to 109.6% is acceptable, which sheds light on the fact that our methodology possesses reasonable dependability of the sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of quenching mechanism for hematin
ce:section.ce:para:
In broad terms, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching encompasses energy transfer, electron transfer, and inner filter effects, and so on. As evident form Fig. 7
B and Table S2, it is quite clear to observe that there exists no variation in the fluorescence lifetime of the prepared NCl-CDs in the presence and absence of hematin, which indicates that the fluorescence quenching of NCl-CDs is not owing to the energy transfer. Conversely, the recording of the redox potential of NCl-CDs was made in Fig. S9. We have discovered the fact that the prepared NCl-CDs manifested a clear reduction potential (Ered = −0.36 V) but no apparent oxidation behaviour. Accordingly, the calculation of the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) and the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) energy levels of the prepared NCl-CDs was carried out to be −4.04 eV and −7.39 eV, correspondingly. Thereafter, the ELUMO and EHOMO of hematin could be figured out −5.02 eV and −2.15 eV [51]. It is quite difficult to transfer electrons from LUMO of the prepared NCl-CDs to LUMO of hematin (Fig. 7C). That is why there is observed no electron transfer mechanism existing between NCl-CDs and hematin.

For the purpose of further studying the mechanism of hematin quenching the prepared NCl-CDs, the measurement of the excitation spectra, together with the emission spectra of the prepared NCl-CDs and the absorption spectra of hematin, was carried out in Fig. 7A. Obviously, there is a rich spectra overlap existing between the absorption spectrum of hematin and the excitation and emission spectra of the prepared NCl-CDs. That is the reason that we speculate that hematin quench the fluorescence intensity of NCl-CDs is owing to IFE. In the meantime, the IFE can be estimated on the basis of the following formula equation:
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d
A
E x
1 −
10
− d
A
E x
·
s
A
E m
10
g
A
E m
1 −
10
− s
A
E m
wherein Fobsd indicates the topmost fluorescence intensity; Fcor suggests the corrected fluorescence intensity, the fluorescence intensity after the removal of the IFE; AEx and AEm both are the representations of the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (λEx = 370 nm) as well as the maximum emission wavelength (λEm = 460 nm), correspondingly.; d denotes the width of the quartz cell (d = 1.00 cm); g indicates the spot thickness, which is formed by the excitation light to the quartz cell (g = 0.40 cm); s suggests the thickness of excitation light (s = 0.10 cm). As presented in Table S3, CF was that Fcor/Fobsd, which could be calculated in accordance with the above equation. Fcor,0 is the corrected fluorescence intensities of NCl-CDs in the absence of hematin; for the purpose of ensuring the reliability of the rectification, the topmost value of CF was not possible to be more than 3. Fcor indicated the fluorescence intensity of NCl-CDs with the varying hematin concentrations subsequent to IFE rectification, and its calculation could be performed in accordance with the above equation. Fcor,o which is 816.8, indicates the PL intensity of NCl-CDs without hematin after IFE correction, as presented in Table S3. Fcor,o/Fcor could reflect the level of quenching subsequent to the IFE rectification. Moreover, the ratio closer to 1 indicates the larger impact of the IFE. E% (E = 1 - F/F0) indicated the suppressed efficiency, whereas F0 and F both were the PL intensity of NCl-CDs when hematin is present and absent, correspondingly. Besides that, we discovered the fact that almost each of the suppressed impacts stemmed from the IFE of hematin (Fig. 7D). Following the removal of the IFE, approximately there did not exist any quenching in the linear range. That is why almost each of the quench impacts stems from the IFE of hematin.

In summary, the fluorescence system, based on NCl-CDs, for the detection of hematin and temperature was put forward. The NCl-CDs, being a productive temperature indicator, manifests reasonable temperature-dependent fluorescence with a sensational linear response between 20 and 80 ℃. In the meantime, this sensitive sensing platform had the potential of showing outstanding analytical efficiency for the detection of hematin with a linear response that ranged between 1.0 μM and 32.0 μM, in addition to a detection limit of 0.16 μM that could be further put to use for investigating the human serum specimens.

Wei He: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Zhenxiang Huo: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xiangying Sun: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Jiangshan Shen: Data curation, Formal analysis, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There does not exist any conflict requiring declaration.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The current research work received the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21275059 and 21575044), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (No. 2015J01054 and 2016J01062), Foundation of Graphene Powder & Composite Research Center of Fujian Province (No. 2017H2001), and Subsidized Project for Postgraduates’ Innovative Fund in Scientific Research of Huaqiao University (No. 18011081004).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2019.104528.
